title: NCCAH: The Metabolic Trap & Classic CAH Reclassification
subtitle: Trainee Errors in Hyperandrogenism Management · Biochemical Phenotyping of "Late-Onset" CAH
authors: Clinical case vignette — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Hyperandrogenism — hirsutism, oligomenorrhea/amenorrhea, trainee management errors
pmh: Vignette 1 (Ms. A): NCCAH × 1 year, prednisolone 5 mg daily
Vignette 2 (Ms. P): provisional NCCAH/PCOS label, severe virilization

caseDescription:
**Vignette 1 — The Metabolic Trap:** Ms. A, 20-year-old female with NCCAH diagnosed one year ago (severe cystic acne, hirsutism Ferriman-Gallwey 16, oligomenorrhea). On prednisolone 5 mg daily with mild acne improvement but persistent hirsutism, irregular menses (35–60 days), 7 kg weight gain, BMI 24→26.5, rising triglycerides. Trainee proposes increasing Wysolone to 7.5 mg and adding bioidentical estradiol + micronized progesterone HRT.

**Vignette 2 — The Late-Onset Illusion:** Ms. P, 26-year-old female with severe hyperandrogenism, primary/secondary amenorrhea, significant virilization. Provisionally labeled NCCAH or severe PCOS because of adult presentation. LC-MS/MS: cortisol 2.98 µg/dL, 17-OHP 89.10 ng/mL, 21-deoxycortisol 10.30 µg/L, testosterone 296 ng/dL, aldosterone 19.1 ng/dL. Trainee plans OCP/anti-androgens only, assuming NCCAH with intact cortisol reserve.
keyFindings: Vignette 1: Iatrogenic metabolic worsening on supraphysiologic glucocorticoid monotherapy for cosmetic hirsutism
Vignette 1: HRT supplements estrogen; OCPs suppress LH and raise SHBG — mechanistically distinct
Vignette 1: Anti-androgenic progestins (drospirenone, CPA, dienogest) + OCP mandatory before spironolactone (teratogenic)
Vignette 2: Basal cortisol 2.98 µg/dL = hypocortisolism — excludes NCCAH (eucortisolism)
Vignette 2: 17-OHP 89 ng/mL and elevated 21-deoxycortisol = Classic Simple Virilizing CAH
Vignette 2: Normal aldosterone 19.1 ng/dL explains survival without neonatal salt-wasting crisis
Vignette 2: Glucocorticoid replacement obligatory — not optional suppression
finalDiagnosis: Vignette 1: NCCAH with trainee trap — glucocorticoid-sparing strategy (low-dose GC + anti-androgenic OCP ± spironolactone). Vignette 2: Classic Simple Virilizing 21-hydroxylase deficiency CAH mislabeled as NCCAH — requires life-saving glucocorticoid replacement plus adjuvant OCP/anti-androgens.
learningObjectives: Avoid dose-escalating glucocorticoids for cosmetic hirsutism in NCCAH — add glucocorticoid-sparing peripheral anti-androgens
Distinguish HRT (supplementation) from OCP (HPO-axis suppression + SHBG induction) in hyperandrogenic NCCAH
Select anti-androgenic progestins and recognize OCP contraceptive safety net before spironolactone
Use basal cortisol, 17-OHP, and 21-deoxycortisol to reclassify "adult-onset" virilization as Classic vs Non-Classic CAH
Recognize Simple Virilizing CAH: hypocortisolism with preserved aldosterone — delayed diagnosis ≠ NCCAH

items.title: Case snapshot: NCCAH on prednisolone with metabolic deterioration
items.content:
Ms. A, 20-year-old, NCCAH × 1 year (cystic acne, FG hirsutism 16, oligomenorrhea).
On prednisolone 5 mg daily: mild acne improvement, persistent hirsutism, menses 35–60 days.
7 kg weight gain, BMI 24→26.5, rising triglycerides.
Trainee error: increase Wysolone to 7.5 mg + bioidentical HRT for menses.
keyPoints: Cosmetic hirsutism persisting on 5 mg prednisolone.
Metabolic penalty emerging — weight, lipids.
Two trainee errors: GC dose escalation and HRT instead of OCP.
Correct pivot: GC-sparing agents + anti-androgenic OCP.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette: The Metabolic Trap in NCCAH: "While her acne has mildly improved, she remains distressed by the facial hirsutism. Her menses are still irregular... she has gained 7 kg over the past year."

items.question: Why is increasing Wysolone to 7.5 mg to control hirsutism a dangerous clinical trap?
items.answer: This is the most common error in managing hyperandrogenism in NCCAH — using a systemic HPA-axis suppressing drug to treat a localized cosmetic issue while ignoring the metabolic penalty. While prednisolone treats root pathophysiology by suppressing ACTH, the dose required to fully clear severe hirsutism as monotherapy often exceeds physiological glucocorticoid requirement. Pushing to 7.5 mg plunges the patient further into iatrogenic Cushingoid features, accelerating weight gain and insulin resistance. Strategy must shift to adding a glucocorticoid-sparing agent for peripheral androgens, titrating Wysolone to the lowest effective dose.
keyPoints: GC monotherapy for hirsutism often requires supraphysiologic doses.
7.5 mg worsens iatrogenic Cushingoid metabolic effects.
Add glucocorticoid-sparing peripheral anti-androgens.
Titrate GC to lowest effective ACTH-suppressing dose.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 1: "Pushing the dose to 7.5 mg will plunge the patient further into iatrogenic Cushingoid features, accelerating her weight gain and insulin resistance. Instead of increasing the glucocorticoid, the strategy must shift to adding a glucocorticoid-sparing agent."

items.question: Why is standard HRT contraindicated in NCCAH hyperandrogenism, and why must an OCP be used instead?
items.answer: HRT is for supplementation — replacing missing estrogen (e.g., primary ovarian insufficiency) with physiological doses that do not adequately suppress the HPO axis. OCPs are for suppression: NCCAH patients often develop secondary polycystic ovarian morphology because adrenal androgens disrupt HPO feedback, driving up LH. OCPs potently suppress LH, shutting down this secondary ovarian androgen factory. HRT cannot achieve this central suppression.
keyPoints: HRT = physiological estrogen replacement, no HPO suppression.
NCCAH: adrenal androgens → secondary PCOM → elevated LH.
OCP suppresses LH and ovarian androgen production.
HRT fails to address secondary ovarian hyperandrogenism.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 2: "HRT is for Supplementation... OCPs are for Suppression: NCCAH patients often develop secondary polycystic ovarian morphology because adrenal androgens disrupt the HPO feedback loop, driving up LH. OCPs potently suppress LH."

items.question: How does OCP estrogen alter peripheral androgen kinetics in a way HRT cannot?
items.answer: OCPs contain synthetic ethinyl estradiol with massive first-pass hepatic effect, inducing logarithmic synthesis of SHBG. Bioidentical estradiol in HRT causes only minimal SHBG increases. Free Androgen Index: FAI = (Total Testosterone / SHBG) × 100. Aggressively driving up SHBG (denominator) is the most efficient way to trap free androgens and crash peripheral active testosterone availability. OCPs achieve this; HRT does not.
keyPoints: Ethinyl estradiol >> bioidentical estradiol for SHBG induction.
Higher SHBG lowers free androgen index mathematically.
Hepatic first-pass effect is key OCP mechanism.
HRT cannot replicate this androgen trapping.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 3: "OCPs contain synthetic estrogen (ethinyl estradiol), which exerts a massive first-pass effect on the liver, inducing logarithmic synthesis of Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin (SHBG). Bioidentical estradiol in HRT causes only minimal SHBG increases."

items.question: How does progestin selection impact treatment of severe hirsutism in NCCAH?
items.answer: Not all progestins are structurally equal. Progestogens in HRT (dydrogesterone, micronized progesterone) are androgen-neutral. For severe hyperandrogenism, select an OCP with anti-androgenic progestin — drospirenone, cyproterone acetate, or dienogest. These molecules actively antagonize the androgen receptor at the pilosebaceous unit, providing localized blockade against adrenal and ovarian androgens that glucocorticoid alone cannot offer.
keyPoints: Micronized progesterone/dydrogesterone are androgen-neutral.
Anti-androgenic progestins: drospirenone, CPA, dienogest.
Direct AR antagonism at pilosebaceous unit.
Complements low-dose glucocorticoid ACTH suppression.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 4: "For severe hyperandrogenism, you must select an OCP formulated with an anti-androgenic progestin (such as drospirenone, cyproterone acetate, or dienogest). These specific molecules actively antagonize the androgen receptor directly at the pilosebaceous unit."

items.question: What hidden safety net does the OCP provide before adding spironolactone?
items.answer: Spironolactone is a pure peripheral anti-androgen and highly teratogenic — it will feminize a male fetus if the patient becomes pregnant. HRT provides highly unreliable to zero contraception. OCPs provide highly effective continuous contraceptive safety net. OCP is mandatory baseline requirement before initiating potent teratogenic anti-androgens in a female of reproductive age.
keyPoints: Spironolactone is teratogenic (fetal feminization).
HRT does not provide reliable contraception.
OCP mandatory before spironolactone in reproductive-age women.
Contraception + anti-androgenic progestin dual benefit.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 5: "Spironolactone is a pure peripheral anti-androgen and is highly teratogenic... OCPs provide a highly effective, continuous contraceptive safety net. This makes the OCP a mandatory baseline requirement before initiating potent teratogenic anti-androgens."

items.question: Ms. A, 20, with NCCAH on prednisolone 5 mg has persistent hirsutism and 7 kg weight gain. Trainee proposes Wysolone 7.5 mg. Best correction?
options: Agree — higher GC dose is first-line for hirsutism in NCCAH
Add glucocorticoid-sparing anti-androgenic OCP; titrate GC to lowest effective dose — avoid supraphysiologic GC for cosmetic hirsutism
Stop all glucocorticoids immediately without replacement
Replace prednisolone with fludrocortisone monotherapy
items.explanation: Dose-escalating GC for hirsutism causes iatrogenic Cushingoid metabolic harm. Add peripheral anti-androgens (anti-androgenic OCP ± spironolactone) and keep GC at lowest ACTH-suppressing dose.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 1: "Instead of increasing the glucocorticoid, the strategy must shift to adding a glucocorticoid-sparing agent that handles the peripheral androgens, allowing the Wysolone to be titrated down to the lowest effective dose."

items.question: To regulate menses in NCCAH with secondary ovarian hyperandrogenism, why is bioidentical HRT (estradiol + micronized progesterone) wrong?
options: HRT causes adrenal crisis in NCCAH
HRT supplements estrogen without suppressing LH-driven secondary ovarian androgen production — OCP needed for HPO suppression
Micronized progesterone is always contraindicated in NCCAH
HRT raises SHBG more than ethinyl estradiol OCPs
items.explanation: HRT = physiological replacement without HPO suppression. NCCAH with elevated LH from adrenal androgen disruption needs OCP to suppress ovarian androgen factory and raise SHBG.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 2: "HRT is for Supplementation... OCPs are for Suppression... OCPs potently suppress LH, shutting down this secondary ovarian androgen factory. HRT cannot achieve this."

items.question: Which OCP feature most efficiently lowers free androgen index in NCCAH?
options: Bioidentical estradiol causing minimal SHBG change
Ethinyl estradiol inducing massive hepatic SHBG synthesis, reducing FAI = Total Testosterone/SHBG × 100
Androgenic progestins increasing testosterone production
Continuous progestin-only pill without estrogen
items.explanation: Ethinyl estradiol first-pass hepatic effect drives SHBG up logarithmically, crashing free androgen index. Bioidentical HRT estradiol cannot achieve this.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 3: "aggressively driving up the denominator (SHBG) is the most efficient mathematical way to trap free androgens and crash the peripheral availability of active testosterone. OCPs achieve this; HRT does not."

items.question: For severe hirsutism in NCCAH, which progestin class should be selected in the OCP?
options: Androgen-neutral micronized progesterone only
Anti-androgenic progestin (drospirenone, cyproterone acetate, or dienogest)
Norethindrone — most androgenic progestin available
Progestin-free estrogen-only HRT
items.explanation: Anti-androgenic progestins antagonize androgen receptor at pilosebaceous unit — localized blockade complementing low-dose glucocorticoid. HRT progestogens are androgen-neutral.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 4: "you must select an OCP formulated with an anti-androgenic progestin (such as drospirenone, cyproterone acetate, or dienogest)."

items.question: Ms. A on low-dose Wysolone + drospirenone OCP has regular menses but stubborn hirsutism. Before spironolactone 100 mg daily, what is mandatory?
options: Discontinue OCP to avoid progestin interaction
Confirm effective contraception via OCP — spironolactone is teratogenic
Increase Wysolone to 15 mg instead of spironolactone
Switch to HRT for better hirsutism control
items.explanation: Spironolactone feminizes male fetus if pregnancy occurs. OCP provides contraceptive safety net; HRT does not. OCP mandatory before teratogenic anti-androgens.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 5: "OCPs provide a highly effective, continuous contraceptive safety net. This makes the OCP a mandatory baseline requirement before initiating potent teratogenic anti-androgens."

items.statement: Increasing prednisolone to 7.5 mg is the preferred strategy to clear severe hirsutism in NCCAH without metabolic consequences.
items.explanation: False. Dose required for hirsutism clearance as GC monotherapy often exceeds physiological requirement → iatrogenic Cushingoid weight gain and insulin resistance. Add glucocorticoid-sparing agents instead.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 1: "Pushing the dose to 7.5 mg will plunge the patient further into iatrogenic Cushingoid features, accelerating her weight gain and insulin resistance."

items.statement: Bioidentical HRT and combined OCPs are interchangeable for regulating menses and suppressing hyperandrogenism in NCCAH.
items.explanation: False. HRT supplements estrogen without HPO suppression or significant SHBG induction. OCPs suppress LH and raise SHBG — mechanistically distinct and required for hyperandrogenic NCCAH.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 2: "The trainee fundamentally misunderstands the pharmacodynamic intent of the two therapies."

items.assertion: An anti-androgenic OCP should be added to low-dose glucocorticoid in NCCAH with persistent hirsutism.
items.reason: OCPs suppress LH-driven secondary ovarian androgens and induce SHBG to lower free androgen index.
items.explanation: Both true; OCP addresses peripheral and ovarian androgens GC monotherapy cannot clear without supraphysiologic dosing.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Questions 2–3: "OCPs potently suppress LH, shutting down this secondary ovarian androgen factory" and "inducing logarithmic synthesis of Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin (SHBG)."

items.title: Case snapshot: 26-year-old with "NCCAH" label and Classic SV-CAH biochemistry
items.content:
Ms. P, 26-year-old, severe hyperandrogenism, amenorrhea, significant virilization.
Provisionally labeled NCCAH or severe PCOS due to adult presentation.
LC-MS/MS: cortisol 2.98 µg/dL, 17-OHP 89.10 ng/mL, 21-deoxycortisol 10.30 µg/L, testosterone 296 ng/dL, aldosterone 19.1 ng/dL.
Trainee plans OCP/anti-androgens only — misses obligatory glucocorticoid replacement.
keyPoints: Adult presentation ≠ NCCAH onset.
Cortisol 2.98 µg/dL = frank hypocortisolism.
17-OHP 89 ng/mL + elevated 21-deoxycortisol = Classic CAH.
Normal aldosterone = Simple Virilizing, not salt-wasting.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette: The "Late-Onset" Illusion: "Because she is presenting in adulthood, she has been provisionally labeled by the referring physician as having severe Non-Classic Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (NCCAH) or severe Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)."

items.question: Why is clinical presentation age a dangerous diagnostic anchor for NCCAH vs Classic CAH?
items.answer: Age of diagnosis does not equal age of onset. NCCAH manifests biochemically in late childhood/puberty. Classic Simple Virilizing CAH is a profound congenital defect present at birth. In female infants, SV-CAH causes ambiguous genitalia — but clitoromegaly or labial fusion may be missed, ignored, or surgically addressed without endocrine workup. Patient survives childhood without salt wasting but reaches adulthood severely virilized. Trainee confuses delayed diagnosis of severe congenital defect with delayed onset of mild defect.
keyPoints: Diagnosis age ≠ disease onset age.
SV-CAH congenital — may be missed in infancy.
Missed genital ambiguity → undiagnosed until adulthood.
Severe virilization in adult ≠ automatically NCCAH.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 1: "Age of diagnosis does not equal age of onset... The trainee is confusing a delayed diagnosis of a severe congenital defect with a delayed onset of a mild defect."

items.question: How does basal cortisol 2.98 µg/dL dismantle the NCCAH diagnosis?
items.answer: NCCAH = partial enzyme deficiency (typically 20–50% retained CYP21A2 activity). Pituitary pushes enough ACTH to overcome mild block → eucortisolism (normal baseline cortisol). Morning cortisol 2.98 µg/dL represents overt hypocortisolism. Enzyme block so severe (typically 1–2% retained activity) that even maximum ACTH drive cannot synthesize adequate glucocorticoids. NCCAH patients do not have baseline cortisol this low — confirms Classic phenotype.
keyPoints: NCCAH: partial block → eucortisolism.
Cortisol 2.98 µg/dL = overt hypocortisolism.
~1–2% enzyme activity in Classic SV-CAH.
Single cortisol value excludes NCCAH.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 2: "A morning cortisol of 2.98 µg/dL represents overt hypocortisolism... A patient with NCCAH does not have a baseline cortisol this low. This confirms a Classic phenotype."

items.question: How do 17-OHP thresholds separate Non-Classic from Classic CAH?
items.answer: 17-OHP is primary substrate measuring backlog before blocked 21-hydroxylase enzyme. NCCAH: basal levels usually 2–10 ng/mL; even after ACTH stimulation rarely exceed 20–30 ng/mL. Classic CAH: basal levels massively elevated, almost always >30 ng/mL, frequently >100 ng/mL. Basal 89 ng/mL indicates absolute structural bottleneck — definitively Classic category.
keyPoints: NCCAH basal 17-OHP: 2–10 ng/mL.
Classic basal 17-OHP: >30 ng/mL, often >100 ng/mL.
89 ng/mL = Classic CAH.
17-OHP measures substrate backlog at enzyme block.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 3: "NCCAH: Basal levels are usually between 2 ng/mL and 10 ng/mL... Classic CAH: Basal levels are typically massively elevated, almost always >30 ng/mL, and frequently >100 ng/mL. A basal level of ~89 ng/mL indicates an absolute structural bottleneck."

items.question: What is the mechanistic significance of elevated 21-deoxycortisol in severe 21-hydroxylase deficiency?
items.answer: 21-Deoxycortisol is pathognomonic footprint of severe CYP21A2 block. When primary pathway (17-OHP → 11-deoxycortisol) is completely blocked, massive 17-OHP accumulation seeks bypass valve — shunted to CYP11B1 (11β-hydroxylase), which converts 17-OHP directly to 21-deoxycortisol. Bypass pathway is highly inefficient; significant 21-deoxycortisol elevations occur only with near-total 21-hydroxylase block (Classic CAH). Rarely elevated in heterozygotes or mild NCCAH.
keyPoints: 21-deoxycortisol = severe CYP21A2 block marker.
Bypass: 17-OHP → CYP11B1 → 21-deoxycortisol.
Elevated only in near-total enzyme deficiency.
Confirms Classic over NCCAH phenotype.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 4: "21-Deoxycortisol is the pathognomonic footprint of a severe CYP21A2 block... significant elevations of 21-Deoxycortisol only occur when the 21-hydroxylase block is near-total (Classic CAH)."

items.question: Why did this hypocortisolemic patient not die of Addisonian crisis in infancy?
items.answer: Aldosterone 19.1 ng/dL is robust and entirely normal. This is dividing line between Salt-Wasting vs Simple Virilizing Classic CAH. In Simple Virilizing CAH, tiny fraction residual enzyme activity (~1–2%) — just enough to synthesize mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) in zona glomerulosa, but not enough cortisol in zona fasciculata. Intact aldosterone prevented neonatal salt-wasting crisis, allowing survival into adulthood undiagnosed.
keyPoints: Normal aldosterone = no neonatal salt wasting.
SV-CAH: ~1–2% activity — aldosterone yes, cortisol no.
Salt-wasting form has insufficient mineralocorticoid synthesis.
Survival to adulthood with missed virilization diagnosis.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 5: "The patient's Aldosterone is 19.1 ng/dL, which is robust and entirely normal... just enough to synthesize mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) in the zona glomerulosa, but not enough to synthesize adequate cortisol in the zona fasciculata."

items.question: How does biochemical reclassification change management from the trainee's OCP-only plan?
items.answer: Trainee assumed intrinsic cortisol reserve (NCCAH) and planned OCP/anti-androgens only. Labs prove Classic SV-CAH with frank hypocortisolism — exogenous glucocorticoids (Wysolone or dexamethasone) are no longer optional suppressive therapies but obligatory life-saving replacement. Withholding glucocorticoids leaves cortisol 2.98 µg/dL → immediate lethal adrenal crisis risk with physiological stress (illness, trauma, surgery). OCPs remain useful adjuvants for symptom control; systemic glucocorticoid replacement is non-negotiable.
keyPoints: Classic SV-CAH: GC = replacement, not optional suppression.
Cortisol 2.98 without replacement = crisis risk.
Stress doses mandatory education.
OCP/anti-androgens adjuvant, not sole therapy.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 6: "exogenous glucocorticoids (like Wysolone or Dexamethasone) are no longer optional suppressive therapies—they are obligatory, life-saving replacement therapies... placing her at immediate, lethal risk of an adrenal crisis."

items.question: A 26-year-old with virilization is labeled "adult-onset NCCAH" because diagnosis occurred in adulthood. Best critique of this anchor?
options: NCCAH never occurs after childhood
Age of diagnosis ≠ age of onset — Classic SV-CAH may be missed in infancy and present severely virilized in adulthood
All adult hyperandrogenism is PCOS until proven otherwise
Virilization in adults excludes all forms of CAH
items.explanation: Classic SV-CAH is congenital; missed ambiguous genitalia/clitoromegaly in infancy allows survival without salt wasting but delayed diagnosis. Confusing delayed diagnosis with mild late-onset NCCAH is dangerous.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 1: "The trainee is confusing a delayed diagnosis of a severe congenital defect with a delayed onset of a mild defect."

items.question: Morning cortisol 2.98 µg/dL in a patient suspected of NCCAH. Most accurate interpretation?
options: Consistent with NCCAH eucortisolism
Overt hypocortisolism confirming Classic CAH — NCCAH retains normal basal cortisol
Normal variant requiring no glucocorticoid
Artifact requiring only repeat testing without treatment
items.explanation: NCCAH partial block → eucortisolism. Cortisol 2.98 µg/dL = severe enzyme deficiency (~1–2% activity) with inadequate glucocorticoid synthesis despite ACTH drive.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 2: "In NCCAH, the pituitary pushes enough ACTH to overcome the mild block, resulting in eucortisolism (normal baseline cortisol). A morning cortisol of 2.98 µg/dL represents overt hypocortisolism."

items.question: Basal 17-OHP 89 ng/mL. How does this classify CAH severity?
options: Borderline NCCAH — observe without treatment
Classic CAH — basal >30 ng/mL, frequently >100 ng/mL; NCCAH basal usually 2–10 ng/mL
Normal — 17-OHP is only interpretable after ACTH stimulation
Excludes 21-hydroxylase deficiency
items.explanation: 89 ng/mL basal 17-OHP = massive substrate backlog at blocked 21-hydroxylase → Classic CAH. NCCAH rarely exceeds 20–30 ng/mL even post-stimulation.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 3: "Classic CAH: Basal levels are typically massively elevated, almost always >30 ng/mL, and frequently >100 ng/mL. A basal level of ~89 ng/mL indicates an absolute structural bottleneck."

items.question: Markedly elevated 21-deoxycortisol on LC-MS/MS steroid panel indicates:
options: Mild heterozygote carrier state
Near-total CYP21A2 block with bypass shunting of 17-OHP via CYP11B1 — Classic CAH footprint
Primary adrenal insufficiency unrelated to CAH
PCOS-related adrenal hyperandrogenism only
items.explanation: 21-deoxycortisol elevation is pathognomonic of severe 21-hydroxylase block via inefficient bypass pathway. Rarely significant in NCCAH or heterozygotes.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 4: "21-Deoxycortisol is the pathognomonic footprint of a severe CYP21A2 block... significant elevations of 21-Deoxycortisol only occur when the 21-hydroxylase block is near-total (Classic CAH)."

items.question: Classic CAH patient with cortisol 2.98 µg/dL has aldosterone 19.1 ng/dL (normal). This explains:
options: Salt-wasting crisis in infancy was inevitable
Simple Virilizing Classic CAH — sufficient residual activity for mineralocorticoids but not adequate cortisol
Complete absence of all adrenal steroid synthesis
Diagnosis of primary hyperaldosteronism
items.explanation: SV-CAH: ~1–2% enzyme activity synthesizes aldosterone in zona glomerulosa but insufficient cortisol in zona fasciculata. Normal aldosterone prevented neonatal salt-wasting death.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 5: "In Simple Virilizing CAH, there is a tiny fraction of residual enzyme activity (approx. 1-2%)—just enough to synthesize mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) in the zona glomerulosa, but not enough to synthesize adequate cortisol."

items.question: Ms. P (cortisol 2.98 µg/dL, 17-OHP 89 ng/mL) — trainee plans OCP monotherapy. Correct management?
options: OCP only — glucocorticoids contraindicated in CAH
Obligatory glucocorticoid replacement (life-saving) + OCP/anti-androgens as adjuvants for hyperandrogenism
Anti-androgens only until pregnancy desired
Fludrocortisone monotherapy without glucocorticoid
items.explanation: Classic SV-CAH with hypocortisolism requires mandatory GC replacement — crisis risk with stress. OCP/anti-androgens adjuvant for virilization/hirsutism, not replacement for cortisol deficiency.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 6: "exogenous glucocorticoids... are obligatory, life-saving replacement therapies... OCPs can still be used as an adjuvant for symptom control, but systemic glucocorticoid replacement is non-negotiable."

items.statement: A basal morning cortisol of 2.98 µg/dL is compatible with a diagnosis of Non-Classic CAH.
items.explanation: False. NCCAH maintains eucortisolism from partial enzyme block. Cortisol 2.98 µg/dL is overt hypocortisolism confirming Classic phenotype.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 2: "A patient with NCCAH does not have a baseline cortisol this low. This confirms a Classic phenotype."

items.statement: Elevated 21-deoxycortisol on LC-MS/MS is a pathognomonic marker of near-total 21-hydroxylase deficiency in Classic CAH.
items.explanation: True. Massive 17-OHP shunted via CYP11B1 bypass to 21-deoxycortisol only with near-total block — rarely elevated in NCCAH.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 4: "21-Deoxycortisol is the pathognomonic footprint of a severe CYP21A2 block."

items.statement: In Classic Simple Virilizing CAH with hypocortisolism, OCP monotherapy without glucocorticoid replacement is safe outpatient management.
items.explanation: False. Frank hypocortisolism without replacement places patient at lethal adrenal crisis risk with physiological stress. GC replacement non-negotiable.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 6: "If the trainee withheld glucocorticoids and relied only on OCPs, the patient would be left with a cortisol of 2.98 µg/dL, placing her at immediate, lethal risk of an adrenal crisis."

items.assertion: Ms. P requires immediate glucocorticoid replacement despite adult presentation.
items.reason: Her basal cortisol of 2.98 µg/dL confirms Classic Simple Virilizing CAH with frank hypocortisolism, not eucortisolemic NCCAH.
items.explanation: Both true; R correctly explains A. Adult age of diagnosis does not override biochemical Classic phenotype with life-threatening cortisol deficiency.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Questions 2 and 6: "A patient with NCCAH does not have a baseline cortisol this low" and "exogenous glucocorticoids... are obligatory, life-saving replacement therapies."

items.assertion: Normal aldosterone in this patient explains absence of neonatal salt-wasting crisis.
items.reason: Simple Virilizing CAH retains ~1–2% enzyme activity sufficient for mineralocorticoid but not glucocorticoid synthesis.
items.explanation: Both true; residual zona glomerulosa function preserves aldosterone while fasciculata cortisol synthesis fails.
items.reference: Teaching Q&A Question 5: "just enough to synthesize mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) in the zona glomerulosa, but not enough to synthesize adequate cortisol in the zona fasciculata."
